summary: Argentine writer and key figure of the Latin American Boom. Best known for the nonlinear novel Hopscotch and a large body of short stories including 'Axolotl,' 'Blow-Up,' 'Bestiary,' 'House Taken Over,' 'The Continuity of Parks,' 'The Night Face Up,' 'Letter to a Young Lady in Paris,' 'The Pursuer,' and 'The Idol of the Cyclades.' Self-exiled to Paris. Translated Edgar Allan Poe's complete prose works into Spanish. Created the fictional creatures famas and cronopios.

works.name: Hopscotch (Rayuela)
works.description: Cortázar's most famous novel, about Horacio Oliveira and his lover La Maga, set partly in Paris and partly in Buenos Aires. Contains 155 chapters, of which the last 99 are 'expendable.' Opens with a 'Table of Instructions' that advises reading in multiple nonlinear orders (starting at chapter 73). Horacio is a member of the bohemian Serpent Club. He confuses Talita (Traveler's wife) with La Maga. Horacio and Traveler work at an insane asylum. The novel includes the literary criticism of a mysterious writer called Morelli, whose musings Cortázar expanded into the novel 62: A Model Kit. Horacio searches for the 'kibbutz of desire.' The novel features a language called Gliglico. A subplot involves Juan's suspicions that Frau Marta is a vampire preying on English tourists. Wikipedia

a novel with 99 'expendable chapters' that can be read in two different sequences.

works.name: "Axolotl"
works.description: A short story in which the narrator visits the Jardin des Plantes in Paris, becomes obsessed with axolotls (which he compares to 'Chinese figurines of milky glass'), and eventually transforms into one. He notes that 'larva means disguise and also phantom.' He is fascinated by their 'golden eyes' and 'terrifying purity.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Narrator obsessively watches axolotls at the Jardin des Plantes and transforms into one
examples: a visitor to the Jardin des Plantes in Paris is fascinated by, and eventually turns into, the title amphibian.
a man who repeatedly watches an aquarium at the Jardin des Plantes undergoes a mental transformation into a fish.
the narrator stares so long at the title creatures in the zoo that he becomes one of them.

clues.clue: 'Golden eyes' / 'eyes of gold' / 'Chinese figurines of milky glass' / 'larva means disguise and also phantom'
examples: the narrator compares these animals to 'Chinese figurines of milky glass' and muses that 'larva means disguise and also phantom.'
obsession with the title creature's 'golden eyes'

works.name: "Blow-Up" ("Las babas del diablo")
works.description: A short story in which the photographer Roberto Michel, while walking along the Seine in Paris and translating a treatise by José Norberto Allende, discovers that a blonde woman was propositioning a teenage boy on behalf of a man waiting in a car (or a man in a grey hat). Michel enlarges a photograph he took of the scene and becomes obsessed/tormented by it. Adapted into a film by Michelangelo Antonioni (in which the photographer discovers a dead body). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Roberto Michel photographs a woman seducing/propositioning a teenage boy near the Seine; enlarges the photo and becomes obsessed
examples: the photographer Roberto Michel, who unwittingly captures a crime on camera.
Roberto Michel agonizes over photos he took of a woman approaching a young boy in a park near the Seine.
observes a woman seducing a teenage boy, and then enlarges a photograph he takes of the scene.

clues.clue: Adapted into a film by Michelangelo Antonioni (photographer discovers a dead body)
examples: A photographer discovers a dead body in the film adaptation of a story by this author directed by Michelangelo Antonioni.

clues.clue: Michel translating a treatise by José Norberto Allende when he takes the photo
examples: working as a translator when he discovers that a blonde woman was propositioning a teenage boy
While translating a treatise by Jose Norberto Allende

works.name: "Bestiary"
works.description: A short story in which Isabel and the Kid spend time at a summer house where a tiger wanders freely through the grounds. The narrator misinforms the Kid about the tiger's location, and the Kid is mauled to death. Rema disapproves of Isabel and Nino keeping ants. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A tiger wanders freely through a house; the Kid is killed after being given wrong information about its location
examples: The Kid is killed by a tiger that wanders freely around the grounds in this author's story 'Bestiary.'
Isabel gives him the wrong info about the whereabouts of a tiger who wanders through their summer house.
the narrator misinforms the Kid about the location of his family's pet tiger and the Kid gets mauled to death.

clues.clue: Narrator angry at Kid's abusive attitude towards Rema
examples: the narrator becomes angry at the Kid's abusive attitude towards Rima

works.name: "House Taken Over" ("Casa Tomada")
works.description: A short story likely allegorizing Perón's government, in which strange noises force the narrator and his knitting-obsessed sister Irene into progressively smaller areas of their home. The narrator buys Irene wool while also buying French literature. They flee and the narrator throws the house key in the sewer. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Strange noises force narrator and sister Irene into smaller areas of their house; narrator throws key in sewer
examples: The narrator throws his house key in the sewer after fleeing his house with his knitting-obsessed sister Irene
strange noises force the narrator and his sister Irene into progressively smaller areas of their home.

clues.clue: Narrator buys Irene wool and French literature
examples: the narrator buys his sister Irene wool while also going to buy French literature.

clues.clue: Likely allegorizes Perón's government
examples: Cortazar likely allegorized Juan Peron's government in this story

works.name: "The Continuity of Parks"
works.description: A metafictional short story in which a man sitting in a green velvet armchair reads a novel about two lovers who plan to murder a man reading a novel in a green velvet armchair. At a cabin, the woman kisses the bloodied face of her knife-wielding lover before they depart on opposite paths. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Man in green velvet armchair reads novel in which two characters come to life to kill its reader (also in a green velvet armchair)
examples: two characters in a novel come to life to kill its reader
a man sitting in a high-backed green velvet chair to read a novel in which another man enters his house to kill him.
a man sitting in a green velvet armchair reads about two lovers' plan to murder a man who is reading a novel while sitting in a green velvet armchair.

works.name: "The Night Face Up"
works.description: A story in which a hospitalized motorcyclist dreams of being a sacrificial victim in the Aztec 'War of the Blossom.' The twist: the motorcycle accident was actually the dream, and the narrator's sacrifice by the Aztecs (Motecas) is real. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Motorcyclist dreams of Aztec sacrifice / 'War of the Blossom'; the motorcycle accident turns out to be the dream
examples: a hospitalized motorcyclist intermittently dreams of being a sacrificial victim.
a motorcycle accident was actually a dream and the narrator's sacrifice by the Aztecs is real.
imagining himself in a motorcycle accident before being put to death in the War of the Blossoms at the hands of the Motecas.

works.name: "Letter to a Young Lady in Paris"
works.description: A story in which the narrator writes letters to Andrea about his bizarre affliction of vomiting rabbits after moving into her apartment. He tries to hide them in an armoire but is eventually driven to suicide by jumping off a balcony/out a window. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Narrator vomits rabbits after moving into girlfriend/friend's apartment; driven to suicide by jumping out the window
examples: convinced to move into his girlfriend's apartment where he starts vomiting rabbits everywhere and is driven to suicide.
a young man who becomes so nervous about moving into his girlfriend's Paris apartment that he starts vomiting bunny rabbits.
this bizarre affliction of his, which intrudes upon the 'compact order' of a house

works.name: "The Pursuer"
works.description: A story dedicated 'In memoriam Ch. P.' (Charlie Parker). Bruno follows the heroin-addicted jazz saxophonist Johnny Carter. Johnny annotates the Dylan Thomas poem 'The Mask' and dies after declaring he wants to give up jazz and move to Mexico. Bruno publishes a recording of 'Amorous.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Dedicated to Charlie Parker; Bruno follows heroin-addicted saxophonist Johnny Carter
examples: dedicated 'In memoriam Ch. P.', in which Bruno follows the heroin-addicted jazz saxophonist Johnny Carter.
dedicated to Charlie Parker a story narrated by Bruno, who publishes a recording of 'Amorous' by the saxophonist Johnny Carter.

clues.clue: Johnny annotates Dylan Thomas poem 'The Mask'; dies after wanting to give up jazz and move to Mexico
examples: annotates the Dylan Thomas poem 'The Mask' and dies after declaring that he wants to give up jazz and move to Mexico.

comprehensive_summary:
Julio Cortázar is an Argentine writer and key figure of the Latin American Boom, overwhelmingly identified in quizbowl by the nonlinear novel Hopscotch and his numerous short stories.

Hopscotch (Rayuela) is his most frequently clued novel. It contains 155 chapters, with the last 99 described as 'expendable.' A 'Table of Instructions' advises the reader to start at chapter 73 and read in various nonlinear orders. The novel follows Horacio Oliveira and his lover La Maga, a member of the bohemian Serpent Club in Paris. Horacio later moves to Buenos Aires, where he confuses Talita (the wife of Traveler) with La Maga and works at an insane asylum with Traveler. A mysterious writer called Morelli contributes literary criticism throughout; Morelli's musings in the 62nd chapter were expanded into the novel 62: A Model Kit. Other details include the language Gliglico, the 'kibbutz of desire,' Osip Gregorovius nearly seducing La Maga, and a scene where everyone knows a baby is dead except the mother.

'Axolotl' is the most frequently clued short story. The narrator visits the Jardin des Plantes in Paris, becomes obsessed with axolotls (comparing them to 'Chinese figurines of milky glass,' noting 'larva means disguise and also phantom'), and eventually transforms into one.

'Blow-Up' features the photographer Roberto Michel, who while walking along the Seine and translating a treatise by José Norberto Allende, photographs a blonde woman propositioning a teenage boy. He enlarges the photograph and becomes tormented by it. Michelangelo Antonioni adapted it into a film in which the photographer discovers a dead body.

'Bestiary' features a tiger that wanders freely through a summer house. The narrator misinforms the Kid about the tiger's location, and the Kid is mauled to death, motivated by the Kid's abusive attitude towards Rema.

'House Taken Over' likely allegorizes Perón's government: strange noises force the narrator and his knitting-obsessed sister Irene into progressively smaller areas of their home. The narrator buys Irene wool and French literature. They flee and he throws the key in the sewer.

'The Continuity of Parks' is a metafictional story: a man in a green velvet armchair reads a novel about two lovers who plan to murder a man reading in a green velvet armchair.

'The Night Face Up' features a motorcyclist hospitalized after an accident who dreams of being an Aztec sacrificial victim in the 'War of the Blossom.' The twist: the motorcycle accident is the dream and the Aztec sacrifice is real.

'Letter to a Young Lady in Paris' features a narrator who vomits rabbits after moving into his friend Andrea's apartment and is driven to suicide by jumping out a window.

'The Pursuer' is dedicated 'In memoriam Ch. P.' (Charlie Parker). Bruno follows the heroin-addicted jazz saxophonist Johnny Carter, who annotates Dylan Thomas's 'The Mask' and dies after wanting to give up jazz.

Other clued stories include 'The Idol of the Cyclades' (archaeologist Morand kills Somoza with a stone hatchet), 'End of the Game' (girls play 'Statues and Attitudes' by train tracks; Ariel leaves notes), 'The Southern Highway' (months-long traffic jam, inspired Godard's Week End), 'The Distances' (Alina Reyes's diary entries, connection to a Budapest beggar), 'Secret Weapons,' 'Reunion' (Che Guevara narrator), and 'Instructions for John Howell.'

Other works include A Manual for Manuel (VIP kidnapped by 'the Screwery'), Cronopios and Famas (fictional creatures), Los Autonautas de la cosmopista (VW trip with Carol Dunlop), and Fantomas versus the Multinational Vampires. Cortázar translated Poe's complete prose works into Spanish. He was pressured to resign his professorship in 1946 and self-exiled to Paris. Alejandra Pizarnik lost a manuscript of his; after her suicide he wrote a poem placing her in 'the garden where Alice was waiting.'
recursive_suggestions: Hopscotch — has appeared as implied in many bonus leadins

Axolotl — very frequently clued, could appear as its own answerline
House Taken Over — has appeared as its own answerline
Bestiary — could appear as its own answerline
Jorge Luis Borges — frequently mentioned alongside Cortázar as Argentine authors
